data.source: Online experiments with participants recruited via Amazon Mechanical Turk and a university student sample
data.sample_size: Study 1A: n=119; Study 2a: n=149; Study 2b: n=127; 2x2x2 factorial study: n=425; Final study: n=108; Other studies: not stated
data.notes: Multiple experimental studies manipulating rating distributions (bimodal vs unimodal), self-concept clarity, context (self-expressive vs functional), and attribute focus (style vs quality). Exact materials and full sample counts for all eight studies not fully detailed in the provided text.

method.description: Across a series of experiments, participants evaluated products presented with different rating distributions (bimodal/polarizing vs unimodal/non-polarizing). The studies measured perceived self-expressiveness, product desirability, and willingness to pay, while manipulating or measuring self-concept clarity, use context (self-expressive vs functional), rated attribute (style vs quality), and identity-expression focus (individual vs group). Analyses included t-tests, regressions with interactions, and moderated mediation using a bootstrapping procedure.
steps: Recruit participants (primarily via Amazon Mechanical Turk; university students in one study).
Present product stimuli (e.g., movies, clothing/shoes) accompanied by manipulated rating distributions (bimodal vs unimodal; additional multimodal variants in some studies).

Manipulate or measure moderators: self-concept clarity (measured or via recall task), use context (self-expressive vs functional), rated attribute (style vs quality), and identity expression focus (individual vs group).
Collect outcome measures: perceived self-expressiveness, product desirability, and willingness to pay.
Conduct statistical analyses: t-tests and linear regressions with interaction terms to test effects and interactions.
Run moderated mediation analyses (bootstrapping) to test whether perceived self-expressiveness mediates effects of polarization under specific contexts/attributes.
method.models: t-tests; linear regression with interaction terms; moderated mediation via bootstrapping (PROCESS procedure)
method.outcome_variable: Perceived self-expressiveness; product desirability; willingness to pay (WTP)
method.independent_variables: Rating distribution (bimodal vs unimodal); self-concept clarity (measured or manipulated); use context (self-expressive vs functional); rated attribute (style vs quality); identity expression focus (individual vs group)
method.tools_software: PROCESS bootstrapping procedure; specific software not stated

results.summary: Across multiple experiments, products with bimodal (polarizing) rating distributions were perceived as more self-expressive and, particularly when self-expression needs were salient (e.g., low self-concept clarity or self-expressive contexts), were evaluated as more desirable and sometimes commanded higher willingness to pay. Effects were stronger when polarization applied to self-expressive attributes (style) rather than functional attributes (quality). Individual-level identity expression increased preference for polarizing products more than group-level identity expression. Moderated mediation analyses showed perceived self-expressiveness mediated these relationships under self-expressive attribute/context conditions.
